context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this reflection about pro vs anti-development. The prompt feels super broad, and I can’t tell if I’m supposed to define anti-development, talk about history, or take a position. Could you help me figure out what the question is really asking?
exchanges.tutor: That feeling of being lost with a broad prompt is totally normal! Here's a question to start: when you read the prompt, which part of it felt most interesting or provocative to you personally? Sometimes that's the best entry point into a reflection piece.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to engage with the core tension in the prompt themselves. I'll acknowledge their feeling, but redirect them to do the intellectual work of identifying what strikes them most about the question. This avoids over-scaffolding and keeps the student in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: Honestly I can’t even tell what counts as “anti-development” here, so I don’t know what I’m reacting to. It all kind of blends together for me with anti-planning, post-development, and just criticizing bad policies.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really important distinction to work out. Let me ask you this: is there a difference between saying 'this development project was badly designed' versus 'development itself is the wrong goal'? What do you think separates those two positions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling to distinguish between related concepts. Rather than defining them, I should ask a Socratic question that pushes the student to think about what the core difference might be between critiquing development and rejecting it altogether. This will help them build their own conceptual distinction, which is foundational to the assignment.

exchanges.student: Yeah maybe, but I’m not confident about it. I feel like if someone criticizes development hard enough, then isn’t that basically anti-development? I’m worried I’m missing the whole conceptual difference.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: someone can criticize how a hospital is run without saying hospitals shouldn't exist. Does that help clarify where the line might be? Try applying that logic to development — what would the equivalent of 'hospitals shouldn't exist' look like in that context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same conceptual uncertainty. Rather than explaining the distinction, I should push them to think about it through a concrete example or analogy, using a Socratic question that forces them to draw a line. This avoids giving the answer while helping them build their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: Sort of, but then I get stuck again because “development” itself seems vague. If development means improvement, then being anti-development sounds absurd, but if it means a specific Western model, then I guess people could reject that. I’m still not sure how I’m supposed to turn that into a reflection instead of just spiraling.
exchanges.tutor: You just landed on something really important — that 'development' isn't a neutral term. Hold onto that. Now, the prompt asks *when* and *why* anti-development arguments emerged. Given what you just said about development as a specific Western model, what historical moment might have made people start questioning that model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually made a significant conceptual breakthrough — they've identified that the meaning of 'development' is contested and that anti-development arguments target a specific model rather than improvement in general. I should affirm this progress without giving the answer, and redirect them toward the assignment by asking them to connect this insight to the prompt's historical question, which will help them start building an argument rather than spiraling.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, that’s where I blank out. Could you maybe just give me the big picture of the historical context, because I feel like I need the whole map before I can say anything at all.
exchanges.tutor: I won't map it out for you — that's your job! But let me ask: from your course readings, what period do you associate with the rise of 'development' as a major global project? Think post-WWII, decolonization, Cold War... does anything from your readings connect to those moments?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide information directly, which would violate the core rules of the assignment. I should refuse to do so, but redirect them productively with a Socratic question that draws on what they likely already know or have read, rather than leaving them completely stranded. I'll prompt them to think about what they've encountered in course readings or general knowledge about when development as a concept became prominent.

exchanges.student: That’s the problem, it all connects and I can’t organize it. Postwar modernization, decolonization, Cold War, neoliberal stuff, post-development critiques — it’s like a pile in my head. I kind of need a simple frame or I’m just going to write something really messy.
exchanges.tutor: You actually just listed the key moments in roughly the right order. That pile in your head is closer to a timeline than you think. Rather than keep talking about it, try this: write 2-3 sentences that put those moments in sequence and explain how each one shifted thinking about development. Don't worry about polish — just get it down and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually named all the relevant historical moments correctly — this is good progress. However, they are stuck in an organizational anxiety loop rather than attempting to write anything. I've been going back and forth on the same conceptual territory for several exchanges. It's time to push the student toward producing written content rather than continuing to talk about it. I should acknowledge that they have the pieces, and ask them to attempt an organization themselves rather than giving them a frame.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I still feel like I’m guessing and I’m worried the whole thing will be built on a wrong frame. I think I need a really basic way to separate the camps first, because right now “pro,” “anti,” and “critical but not anti” all sound the same to me.
exchanges.tutor: We've been circling this for a while now, and I want to be honest with you: you already worked out a meaningful distinction earlier — that anti-development targets a specific model, not improvement itself. That *is* a frame. The worry that you'll get it wrong is stopping you from starting. A messy first attempt is still an attempt. Can you write those 2-3 sentences now, even if they feel uncertain?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still circling the same conceptual anxiety and resisting writing. This is now a clear redundancy pattern across multiple exchanges. Being less Socratic here is warranted — I should be more direct about the fact that we've been going in circles, gently push back, and firmly redirect to writing. I will not give the frame they're asking for, but I'll challenge them to trust what they've already figured out in this conversation.

exchanges.student: I guess, but before I do, can you just tell me if anti-development is basically the same as post-development or not? That one thing is really tripping me up.
exchanges.tutor: That's an interesting nuance, but honestly resolving it won't change much about your reflection — the prompt uses 'anti-development' as its term, so work with that. Don't let one vocabulary question become a roadblock. Write those 2-3 sentences on the historical sequence you named. You can always refine terminology later.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a direct factual question to avoid writing. This is a detour — whether or not anti-development and post-development are identical terms is a nuance that won't substantially change the quality of their reflection piece. I should acknowledge this briefly, flag it as a detour, and firmly redirect to writing. I will not answer the question directly.

exchanges.student: Okay but that’s exactly why I’m stuck — if the terms blur, then I don’t know what argument I’m making. I feel like I need a plain distinction in one or two sentences first or I’m just going to keep mixing everything together.
exchanges.tutor: I'm going to be straight with you: this is the fourth time we've circled back to the same concern, and I'm not going to give you that distinction. The best way to figure out if your terms are blurry is to actually write something and see where it breaks down. Start with one sentence: what do you think anti-development argues? Just one sentence, imperfect is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked essentially the same question about definitional clarity 3-4 times in a row. This is a clear redundancy pattern and the student is using conceptual anxiety as a reason to avoid writing. I should be firm and direct here — not unkind, but clear that this loop needs to stop and that writing is the only way forward. I will not provide the distinction, but I will be honest that continued delay is not productive.

exchanges.student: I’m not trying to avoid it, I’m just genuinely confused. Could you at least say whether anti-development rejects all planning, or whether someone can still be anti-development and plan?
exchanges.tutor: Look at the prompt again — 'Can one be anti-development and still plan?' is literally one of the questions *you* are supposed to answer. Your confusion is the starting point of your argument, not a barrier to it. So: what do *you* think? Take a position, even a tentative one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a question that is actually directly in the prompt: 'Can one be anti-development and still plan?' I should point this out — it's a question they are supposed to answer, not me. This is a good Socratic moment to redirect their confusion back to the assignment itself, and push them to take a position rather than waiting for clarity before writing.
